name: Austria
summary: Strong IT shortage demand and a very cheap English MSc with a clean graduate-to-RWR-Card route are the biggest levers, but a ~10-year citizenship with a near-total dual-citizenship ban, German B1→B2 gates, New Delhi in-person visa processing and a hardening, FPÖ-pressured migration climate make Austria a poor fit for this dual-preferring Bangladeshi couple.

job-market.summary: Data-processing/software engineers on the 2026 nationwide shortage list (64 occupations); ~24k IT deficit, Vienna dev pay ~€58–86k, both placeable.
job-market.reasoning: The Fachkräfteverordnung 2026 (published 23 Dec 2025) expanded the nationwide shortage list to 64 occupations (up from 58 in 2025), with data-processing/high-tech engineers explicitly prioritised, so both engineers fit a route giving up to 20 RWR bonus points, a waived/simplified labour-market test, and faster ~3–4 week processing. Vienna developer pay runs ~€58k average (Glassdoor) to ~€86k senior (SalaryExpert) in 2026, against a reported ~24,000 IT-specialist deficit. English-only roles cluster in Vienna while German widens options, capping this just below the top band.
evidence: Fachkräfteverordnung 2026 (eff. Jan 2026, publ. 23 Dec 2025): 64 nationwide shortage occupations, up from 58 in 2025; data-processing/high-tech engineers prioritised.
Shortage status gives up to 20 RWR points toward the 55-point bar, waives/simplifies the labour-market test, and cuts processing to ~3–4 weeks.
~24,000 IT specialists lacking nationally; Vienna software developer pay ~€58k avg (Glassdoor) to ~€86k senior (SalaryExpert), 2026.
Both spouses are skilled CS graduates, so both can target shortage-occupation employment.

links.title: migration.gv.at – Skilled workers in shortage occupations

visa-access.summary: Residence permits >6 months (incl. student/work) require in-person appearance at the Embassy New Delhi; only D-visa intake runs via VFS Dhaka.
visa-access.reasoning: Per BMEIA New Delhi, applications for residence permits exceeding 6 months, work visas and job-seeker visas must be made in person at the Austrian Embassy New Delhi by Bangladeshi applicants; only the long-stay D-visa intake/biometrics run through VFS in Gulshan, Dhaka, with all decisions made by New Delhi. The MSc residence permit therefore likely involves Delhi travel plus a ~BDT 9,158 VFS service fee, ~€12,000/yr subsistence proof and ~6–8 week processing — real friction versus countries that decide fully in Dhaka, though there is no BD-specific ban.
evidence: BMEIA New Delhi (2026): residence permits >6 months, work visas and job-seeker visas must be applied for in person at the Austrian Embassy New Delhi.
Long-stay D-visa intake lodged via VFS, Gulshan, Dhaka; Schengen C-visas handled by the French Embassy in Dhaka; all assessed by New Delhi.
VFS service charge ~BDT 9,158 incl. VAT per application; visa decision typically 6–8 weeks.
Student residence permit requires admission letter plus proof of funds ~€12,000/year.

links.title: BMEIA – Austrian Embassy New Delhi: how to apply
VFS Global – Austria visa from Bangladesh

citizenship.summary: ~10 years residency, near-total dual-citizenship ban (renounce prior nationality), German B1 set to rise to B2. Deal-breaker for a dual-preferring couple.
citizenship.reasoning: Standard naturalisation requires 10 years' continuous residence (incl. 5 with a settlement permit), and naturalised citizens must generally give up prior citizenship, so both would lose Bangladeshi nationality; The Local (Feb 2026) notes significant reform of the dual-citizenship ban is unlikely as it has deep roots and little political momentum. The ÖVP–SPÖ–NEOS programme plans to raise required German from B1 to B2 for naturalisation, with reform work expected H1 2026; only a 6-year fast track exists, itself needing B2. For a couple who explicitly prefer dual citizenship this is near-disqualifying, despite a world-class passport (visa-free to ~185 destinations, Henley 2026).
evidence: Standard naturalisation after 10 years (incl. 5 with settlement permit); naturalised citizens generally must renounce prior citizenship.
The Local (12 Feb 2026): dual-citizenship reform unlikely soon — deep legal roots, little political momentum; foreign residents call the ban 'a deal breaker' (8 Jan 2026).
Coalition programme plans B1→B2 German + a mandatory ÖIF citizenship course; reform work expected H1 2026 (The Local, 7 Jan 2026).
Upside if naturalised: Austrian passport ~185 visa-free destinations, Henley Index 2026 (~4th–5th globally).

links.title: The Local – Austria's strict dual-citizenship rules ('deal breaker')
The Local – What Austria's planned citizenship reform could mean

post-study-work.summary: 12-month job-seeker extension after graduation, then RWR Card for graduates with no points and no labour-market test — a clean, predictable route.
post-study-work.reasoning: Graduates of Austrian higher-education institutions can renew the residence permit for 12 months to job-hunt or start a business, then switch to a Red-White-Red Card for graduates on a qualification-matched offer at the locally customary graduate salary, with no points system and no AMS labour-market test (migration.gv.at, 2026). The RWR Card carries full labour-market access, so the spouse can move to an RWR Card Plus and work freely. It is clearer and more predictable than relative-points systems, but shorter than the 2–3 year open work permits in Canada/NZ and conditional on landing a qualifying offer.
evidence: Student permit renewable for 12 months post-graduation to seek qualification-matched work or start a business.
RWR Card for graduates: no points system, no labour-market test, at locally customary graduate gross salary (migration.gv.at).
2026 RWR Card 'other key worker' salary floor €3,465/month gross; graduate route benchmarked to comparable Austrian junior pay.
Pathway: 12-mo job search → RWR Card → RWR Card Plus (after ~21 months) → Long-term Resident–EU at 5 years.

links.title: VisaHQ – 2026 RWR / Blue Card salary thresholds

spouse-family.summary: Work-phase spouse works freely (RWR Card Plus, unrestricted labour access); study-phase spouse needs an AMS permit. 2026 reunion freeze is refugee-only.
spouse-family.reasoning: In the work phase the dependant gets a Red-White-Red Card Plus with unrestricted labour-market access (not tied to one employer), strongly fitting a couple who both want to work; the family-reunification freeze extended to 2 July 2026 applies only to recognised refugees and subsidiary-protection holders, NOT to skilled-worker/student family routes, so the prior 'pause risk' was overstated. The main residual friction is the MSc phase: an accompanying spouse on a family-reunification stay permit can work only after an employer obtains an AMS Beschäftigungsbewilligung, and dependents need A1 German and income proof.
evidence: RWR Card Plus gives unrestricted access to the Austrian labour market (employed or self-employed, not employer-bound) — work-phase spouse works freely.
Family-reunification freeze extended to 2 July 2026 applies only to refugees/subsidiary-protection holders, not skilled-worker/student dependents (VisaHQ, Dec 2025).
Study-phase dependants get a 12-month family-reunification stay permit; work needs an AMS permit after a labour-market check.
Dependents must meet A1 German and income requirements; spouse must be 21+ at application.

links.title: VisaHQ – Austria prolongs refugee family-reunification freeze to 2 July 2026

msc-study.summary: English CS MSc at TU Wien/Vienna/TU Graz, very low public tuition €726.72/semester, 20h/week term work (40h in summer); study-phase spouse access restricted.
msc-study.reasoning: Austrian public universities charge non-EU students only €726.72/semester (~€1,453/yr) plus a €26.20 ÖH fee, with multiple fully English-taught CS Master's (University of Vienna, TU Graz, etc., 4 semesters / 120 ECTS) and OeAD scholarships. Non-EU Master's students may work 20h/week during term and up to 40h/week in summer (with an AMS permit). It is excellent value and accessible for both; the residual drag is the AMS-gated study-phase work for the accompanying spouse, keeping it just out of the top band.
evidence: Non-EU public-university tuition €726.72/semester (~€1,453/yr) + €26.20 ÖH fee — far below UK/US/Australia (confirmed univie.ac.at, TU Wien 2026).
Fully English-taught CS Master's at University of Vienna and TU Graz (4 semesters, 120 ECTS); Mastersportal lists many CS/IT Master's.
Third-country Master's students may work 20h/week in term, up to 40h/week in summer (AMS Beschäftigungsbewilligung required).
OeAD scholarships and a central study portal support international applicants; admission letter + ~€12,000/yr funds needed.

links.title: Mastersportal – Computer Science & IT Master's in Austria
WU Vienna – International students: working in Austria (20h/week)

pr-pathway.summary: Long-term Resident–EU after 5 years (RWR + RWR Plus tenure), but needs B1 German (Integration Module 2), income proof; student years count only partially.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Daueraufenthalt-EU is reachable after 5 years of continuous lawful residence combining RWR Card and RWR Card Plus tenure, which is a clear, codified route — the main reason this sits at the top of the 'good' band. It requires Integration Agreement Module 2 (B1 German), proof of stable regular income and a clean record, and student years count only partially toward the 5-year clock, so the realistic PR timeline starts mainly from the work phase. Policy is broadly stable for skilled migrants, though Austria's overall migration framework is tightening.
evidence: Long-term Resident–EU permit after 5 years' continuous lawful residence (RWR + RWR Card Plus tenure combined).
Requires Integration Agreement Module 2 (B1 German) and proof of regular income.
Study-permit years count only partially toward the 5-year clock; clock effectively runs from the work phase.
Route is codified and predictable for skilled workers, but the broader migration framework is tightening in 2026.

income-cost.summary: Two dev salaries (~€58–86k Vienna) but ~40–45% combined tax+social load and ~€1,150 one-bed rents compress net savings to moderate.
income-cost.reasoning: Two software salaries (Vienna dev ~€58k avg to ~€86k senior, 2026) give a solid gross, but ~18% employee social contributions plus progressive income tax leave an effective combined burden around 40–45% at developer income levels, and a Vienna one-bed averages ~€1,150/month (range €730–€1,700) with single living cost ~€1,900/month incl. rent. The 14-pay system (bonus months taxed at flat 6%), a €365 annual transit pass and rent regulation help, and dual income lifts household savings, but the tax-and-rent drag keeps net accumulation mid-band rather than strong.
evidence: Vienna software developer pay ~€58k avg (Glassdoor) to ~€86k senior (SalaryExpert), 2026; entry ~€60k.
Effective combined tax+social burden ~40–45% at developer income; employee social contributions ~18% of gross; 13th/14th salary taxed at flat 6%.
Vienna one-bed rent ~€1,150/month avg (range €730–€1,700); single monthly living cost ~€1,900 incl. rent (Numbeo/Wise, Jun 2026).
Annual public-transport pass €365; rent regulation limits increases — but two engineers face material tax drag.

links.title: Numbeo – Cost of Living in Vienna (Jun 2026)
Wise – Cost of living in Vienna 2026

healthcare.summary: Near-universal statutory insurance (>99% covered), high quality, life expectancy ~81–82, out-of-pocket under ~15%.
healthcare.reasoning: Austria's social health insurance covers over 99% of residents with comprehensive financial protection and out-of-pocket spending under ~15% of total health expenditure (WHO Euro Observatory). Life expectancy is ~81–82 years, above the OECD average, with high physician and hospital-bed density and no formal gatekeeping (direct access to GPs, specialists and hospitals). Both spouses are cheaply covered via employment or student insurance; some non-emergency specialist waits are the only notable gap.
evidence: Near-universal coverage; >99% of population insured through SHI funds (WHO Euro Health Observatory).
Out-of-pocket spending under ~15% of total health spending; cost-sharing exemptions for vulnerable groups.
Life expectancy at birth ~81–82 years, above OECD average; high physician and hospital-bed density.
Direct access to GPs, specialists and hospitals (no gatekeeping); some non-emergency specialist waits.

culture-language.summary: English works in tech and study, but B1 German gates PR, rising-B2 German gates citizenship, and German is needed for officialdom and children's schooling.
culture-language.reasoning: Vienna tech work and English-taught study run fine in English, but German is unavoidable for administration, public services and schooling, and is a hard gate for PR (B1, Integration Agreement Module 2) and citizenship (B1 rising to B2 under the 2025–2029 coalition). Livability is high, but new mandatory integration/values courses and a Dec 2025 headscarf ban for under-14 schoolgirls signal a less accommodating climate, and for a couple targeting long-term settlement the German requirement is a real structural barrier — keeping this mid-band.
evidence: Integration Agreement Module 2 (B1 German) required for Long-term Resident–EU permit.
Citizenship German requirement set to rise from B1 to B2 (coalition plan; The Local, Jan 2026).
English common in Vienna tech roles, but German needed for administration, public services and schooling.
Dec 2025: parliament passed a headscarf ban for schoolgirls under 14 — relevant to integration climate and children's schooling.

links.title: Euronews – Austria passes headscarf ban for schoolgirls under 14

safety-law.summary: Top-tier rule of law (WJP 2025 #12 of 142, score 0.79) and very low crime (Global Peace Index #4); reported crime down 0.2% in 2025.
safety-law.reasoning: Austria ranks 12th globally in the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (overall score 0.79) and 4th on the 2025 Global Peace Index, with low violent crime, reliable courts and trusted public institutions, giving the couple strong day-to-day security and legal protection. Reported crime fell marginally (~0.2%) in 2025 and is mainly property-related. The minor deduction reflects a hardening migration-enforcement and integration-sanctions climate rather than any breakdown in personal safety or judicial fairness.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: Austria 12th globally, overall score 0.79.
Global Peace Index 2025: Austria ranks #4 (score ~1.29); low violent crime, trusted public systems.
Reported crime fell ~0.2% in 2025 vs 2024; top crimes property-related; Vienna safe overall.
Hardening enforcement/integration-sanctions climate is the only meaningful downward pull.

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (full report PDF)
WJP Rule of Law Index – Austria insights

politics.summary: Centrist ÖVP–SPÖ–NEOS coalition since Mar 2025, but polling below majority while FPÖ leads at ~33–35%; migration rules tightening, 3 years of recession.
politics.reasoning: A centrist three-party Stocker coalition (ÖVP–SPÖ–NEOS) took office on 3 Mar 2025 with the FPÖ in opposition, so near-term governance holds, but the coalition now polls below a majority amid internal friction while the FPÖ sits steadily at ~33–35% on a 'remigration' platform — a real medium-term volatility and anti-immigrant risk for Muslim Bangladeshi newcomers. The coalition itself is tightening migration (stricter asylum, B1→B2 German for citizenship, integration sanctions, ~40,000 Syrian status reassessments), against three consecutive years of recession (2023–2025).
evidence: Stocker ÖVP–SPÖ–NEOS coalition sworn in 3 Mar 2025 (first three-party federal govt of the Second Republic); FPÖ in opposition.
2025–2026 polls: ruling parties below a combined majority; FPÖ steady at ~33–35% on 'remigration'.
Coalition tightening migration: stricter asylum, B1→B2 German for citizenship, integration sanctions, ~40,000 Syrian status reassessments.
Three consecutive recession years (2023 −0.7%, 2024 −1.2%, 2025 −0.1%) — worst run of the Second Republic.

tax.summary: Heavy burden: progressive income tax to 55%, ~18% employee social contributions; ~40–45% effective load at developer incomes.
tax.reasoning: Austria's 2026 income tax is progressive from 20% to 55% (top rate above €1m, temporary to 2029), with employees paying ~18% social contributions on top (pension 10.25%, health, unemployment). At typical Vienna developer incomes the effective combined tax-and-social burden is ~40–45%; the flat 6% tax on 13th/14th salaries softens it slightly, but two well-paid engineers keep materially less take-home than in lower-tax destinations, placing this in the weak band.
evidence: 2026 progressive income tax 20–55% (55% above €1m, temporary to 2029); tax-free threshold ~€13,500.
Employee social contributions ~18% of gross (pension 10.25% etc.); effective combined burden ~40–45% at developer income.
13th/14th salary (jahressechstel) taxed at flat 6% — partial relief.
Capital gains/savings (KESt) taxed at 27.5%.

links.title: PwC Worldwide Tax Summaries – Austria (individual income)

muslim-diaspora.summary: Islam recognised since 1912; ~8.3% Muslim (41% of Vienna pupils), abundant mosques/halal incl. a Bangladeshi mosque; but rising hostility, headscarf ban.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Vienna has deep, established Muslim infrastructure — the Vienna Islamic Centre plus many mosques and abundant halal dining, Islam legally recognised since 1912, Muslims ~8.3% nationally (and ~41% of Vienna schoolchildren) — and a specific Bangladeshi community anchor (Bangladesh Islamic Center Baitul Mukarram, plus the Bangladesh Embassy/Permanent Mission in Vienna). But the community base is mainly Turkish/Bosnian rather than South Asian, and rising FPÖ-driven Islamophobia (1,324 recorded anti-Muslim incidents in 2022) plus a Dec 2025 under-14 headscarf ban temper an otherwise strong fit, pulling it just into the upper-mid band.
evidence: Islam recognised by law since 1912; ~8.3% of Austria's population Muslim (Statistik Austria 2021); ~41% of Vienna schoolchildren identify as Muslim.
Vienna Islamic Centre (largest mosque) plus many mosques/prayer rooms and abundant halal dining.
Bangladeshi anchors: Bangladesh Islamic Center Baitul Mukarram (Rudolfsheim-Fünfhaus) and the Bangladesh Embassy/Permanent Mission, Vienna.
Downward pull: 1,324 recorded anti-Muslim incidents in 2022; Dec 2025 ban on headscarves for schoolgirls under 14; FPÖ-driven hostility.

links.title: Euronews – Austria passes headscarf ban for schoolgirls under 14
Embassy & Permanent Mission of Bangladesh, Vienna
